coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric bioassay and visualization of dopamine β-hydroxylase in brain cells utilizing a nanohybrid fluorescence probe
coredata.dc:creator: Amin, Niloufar
Afkhami, Abbas
Hosseinzadeh, Leila
Akbarzadeh, Fatemeh
Madrakian, Tayyebeh
Nabiabad, Haidar Saify
coredata.dc:description: Dopamine β-hydroxylase (DBH) is involved in various neuronal transmission processes in the brain. Due to the severe diseases caused by abnormity levels of such important enzyme in human serum, sensitive and rapid detection of DBH at early stages is crucial, particularly for clinical analysis. Herein, we developed optical sensors for DBH that include the following: (i) a ratiometric fluorescence sensor that hybridizes the bovine serum albumin (BSA)-gold nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) and nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs). The sensor proved to be highly selective and sensitive, achieving a linear range of 0.02–0.16 μg mL−1 and a limit of detection of 4.0 ng mL−1. In the presence of DBH, the fluorescence intensity of BSA-AuNCs (λem = 615 nm) was remarkably quenched by DBH serving as a reporter signal, whereas the N-CDs fluorescence intensity at 440 nm was almost kept unchanged serving as a reference signal. The developed ratiometric sensor is capable of demonstrating a color change from pink to violet and blue with a gradual increase in DBH concentration, which is discernible by the naked-eye. A test strip is prepared for semi-quantitative assay and convenient use. Intriguingly, by taking advantage of the inter-AuNCs aggregation in the presence of DBH, (ii) a resonance light scattering (RLS) sensor was also developed based on the nanohybrid probe (detection limit 95 ng mL−1). Fluorescence imaging in PC12 cell lines demonstrated that the BSA-AuNCs could be utilized in visualization assay towards intracellular DBH. Additionally, the sensors were tested in a real matrix by spiking serum samples with satisfactory recoveries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical sensor
Dopamine β-hydroxylase
Ratiometric fluorescence
Resonance light scattering
Naked-eye detection
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of BSA-AuNCs, N-CDs and BSA-AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid probe
Fluorescence detection of DBH in buffer and serum solution
Preparation of test papers for visual detection of DBH
MTT assay and in vitro fluorescence imaging
Synthesize and characterization of dual emission nanohybrid probe
Application of AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid in detection of DBH enzyme
Selectivity of ratiometric sensor toward DBH
Evaluation of the ratiometric-based sensor in a serum sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image of BSA- AuNCs. (B) N-CDs. (C) BSA- AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid.

(A) FT-IR spectrum of BSA-AuNCs (red line), N-CDs (black line) and BSA- AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid solution (blue line); (B) XPS survey scan of BSA- AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid. Inset: high resolution of Au 4f peaks; (C) Absorption spectra for BSA-AuNCs (red line), N-CDs (blue line) and BSA- AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid (pink line); (D) fluorescence spectra for the BSA-AuNCs (red line), N-CDs (blue line) and BSA- AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid (pink line), Inset: photographs of the BSA-AuNCs, N-CDs and BSA- AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid solutions from left to right respectively under UV light. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article).

(A) Zeta potential measurements of sole BSA-AuNCs, nanohybride probe, BSA-AuNCs- DBH enzyme 100.0 μg/ml at pH 7.0 in aqueous solution. (B) DLS measurements of sole BSA-AuNCs (red line), BSA-AuNCs –DBH enzyme (blue line) 0.5, 2.0 (black line) and 5.0 μg/ml (green line) (C) The effect of pH on the FL intensity ratio (R0/R) of AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid probe before and after the addition of 100.0 μg/ml DBH. (D) Volume ratio dependent FL intensity ratio (R0/R) of nanohybrid probe in the presence of 100.0 ng ml−1 DBH and PB solution of pH 6.0, 20 mM. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric probe upon the exposure to different concentrations of DBH. The pure red BSA-AuNCs (B) plot of fluorescence quenching efficiency (R0/R) of the ratiometric probe as a function of DBH concentrations. PB solution pH 6.0, 20 Mm, time: 2 min (C) The fluorescence photographs of the paper strips embedded with only ratiometric probe in the presence of various amounts of DBH under a 360 nm UV lamp. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article).

(A) Effect of various concentration of DBH on RLS spectra of nanohybrid solution. (B) The linear relationship of RLS intensity response (ΔIRLS) of the nanohybrid versus the different DBH concentrations. (C) The selectivity studies of the ratiometric probe to different possible interfering substances (6000 ng ml−1) in the presence of DBH (100 ng ml−1). Condition: PB solution pH 6.0, 20 mM, time: 2 min. The black bars represent the addition of the sole DBH (100 ng ml−1) to the BSA-AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid solution. The pink-blue bars represent the subsequent addition of various agents (6000 ng ml−1, cu2+: 300 ng ml−1) to the nanohybrid solution containing DBH (100 ng ml−1) (R and R0 were the ratios of the fluorescence intensity of ratiometric probe (F615/F440) in the presence and absence of DBH or different ions and molecules, respectively. (D) Fluorescence images of the ratiometric probe solution upon addition of different possible interfering substances (5000 ng ml−1) under 365 nm UV lamp. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article).

Bright field and fluorescence images of PC12 cells after incubation with BSA-AuNCs (A, B) for 1.5 h and fluorescence image after incubation with BSA-AuNCs/DBH (5.0 μg ml−1) (C). Scale bars: 200 μm [BSA-AuNCs]: 1.050 mg ml−1.
The schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure of BSA-AuNCs/N-CDs and principle detection of DBH enzyme.
Comparison of the proposed method with other methods for the determination of DBH.
Detection of DBH spiked in serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Ratiometric bioassay and visualization of dopamine β-hydroxylase in brain cells utilizing a nanohybrid fluorescence probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Sciences Research Center, School of Pharmacy, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Sciences Research Center, School of Pharmacy, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dopamine β-hydroxylase (DBH) is involved in various neuronal transmission processes in the brain. Due to the severe diseases caused by abnormity levels of such important enzyme in human serum, sensitive and rapid detection of DBH at early stages is crucial, particularly for clinical analysis. Herein, we developed optical sensors for DBH that include the following: (i) a ratiometric fluorescence sensor that hybridizes the bovine serum albumin (BSA)-gold nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) and nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs). The sensor proved to be highly selective and sensitive, achieving a linear range of 0.02–0.16 μg mL−1 and a limit of detection of 4.0 ng mL−1. In the presence of DBH, the fluorescence intensity of BSA-AuNCs (λem = 615 nm) was remarkably quenched by DBH serving as a reporter signal, whereas the N-CDs fluorescence intensity at 440 nm was almost kept unchanged serving as a reference signal. The developed ratiometric sensor is capable of demonstrating a color change from pink to violet and blue with a gradual increase in DBH concentration, which is discernible by the naked-eye. A test strip is prepared for semi-quantitative assay and convenient use. Intriguingly, by taking advantage of the inter-AuNCs aggregation in the presence of DBH, (ii) a resonance light scattering (RLS) sensor was also developed based on the nanohybrid probe (detection limit 95 ng mL−1). Fluorescence imaging in PC12 cell lines demonstrated that the BSA-AuNCs could be utilized in visualization assay towards intracellular DBH. Additionally, the sensors were tested in a real matrix by spiking serum samples with satisfactory recoveries.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine beta hydroxylase (DβH), (EC 1.14.17.1; abbreviated DBH), a catecholamine-synthesizing enzyme, is ascorbic acid (Vit-C) and Cu2+ dependent enzyme [1–3] which catalyzes the hydroxylation reaction of dopamine (DA) into norepinephrine (NE) [1–3]. Since DBH enzyme is particularly expressed in noradrenergic neurons in the central nervous system and adrenal medulla [2,4], any mutations in its structural gene cause changing enzyme activity in plasma while can regulates norepinephrine levels and further influences on neurotransmission in the sympathetic system [4–6]. The abnormity of such a bio and genomarker may cause a dramatic increase in DBH activity and plasma norepinephrine levels in some patients with pheochromocytoma [5]. More importantly, low DBH activity in the locus coeruleus leads to a significant decrease in brain noradrenaline levels which is intensely associated with Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, schizophrenia and DBH deficiency [3,6–9].

According to previous studies, the enzymatic activity of DBH is highly correlated with the plasma level of DBH protein [10,11]. Therefore, the detection of DBH protein is in high demand in the early steps for both clinical diagnosis and pathological elucidation of neurodegenerative disease.

So far, numerous detection techniques have been developed for the detection of DBH such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [10], enzymatic radioassay [12,13], photometric assay [14], high-performance liquid chromatographic (HPLC)-electrochemical method [9,15], dual-wavelength spectrophotometry [16], fluorimetry [17], UHPLC-PDA detection [18]. Current method for DBH assay diagnostic clinicals is ELISA. While ELISA methods need significant instruments, manpower, expense and time that already are not suitable for emergency cases especially in developing countries where medical facilities are unobtainable. However, most of the other sensors suffer from limitations such as application of toxic fluorescence and radioactive reagents, time-consuming procedures, expensive equipment, and need of trained personnel. Therefore, there is a lack of a suitable method to detect the DBH enzyme for clinical and field assays.

Fluorescence techniques have drawn continuing interest in various research fields due to being sensitive, cost-effective, reliable and simple [19]. Particularly, fluorescence shows visualization capabilities for naked-eye detection of analytes under a simple ultraviolet (UV) lamp [19,20]. This feature offers great opportunities in the design and implementation of paper-based fluorescence (bio) sensors which lead to visual detection, portability and on-site response for practical use [21].

Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), one of the most popular fluorescence (FL) probes, are gold nanomaterials comprised of a few to hundreds of gold atoms with ultra-small sizes corresponding to the Fermi wavelength of electrons [22–24]. So far, AuNCs have been used to assay the different important enzymes based on quenching or enhancement of fluorescence intensity at a certain wavelength [23–25].

Up to now, most of the fluorescence-based probes are based on the single modulate type that are limited due to inevitable disturbance induced by fluctuations in instrumental drift (excitation source or detector element) and probe concentration [26–28]. For these reasons, it is difficult to analyze such important biomolecules in a complex real matrix.

Ratiometric fluorescence (RF) -based probes are being implemented to overcome and eliminate those obscurities by measuring the ratio of two emission intensities at different wavelengths. This method has a built-in correction feature that avoids the environmental and background interferences resulting in more sensitive and precise measurements [19,20,26,27,29]. While human eyes are more susceptible to color change over color intensities, RF probes can easily be utilized for qualification and semi-quantification of analytes observed by the naked-eyes [19,20,26,28–31].

The most common design of a ratiometric sensor comprises two different emissive probes in a hybrid system: analyte-responsive fluorophore and a reference fluorophore [26]. Normally, dual emissive FL probe fabricates by using chemical covalent coupling and biological affinity conjugation [20,32].

On the basis of chemical inertness behavior, carbon dots, a class of zero-dimensional carbogenious nanomaterial, are expected to be the proper choice as a sole reference signal which may render further advantages such as high photostability, water solubility, high quantum yield, excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity [29,33,34].

Like CDs, BSA-AuNCs with distinctive optical/colloidal stability, high photostability, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity, hold promising potential in applications ranging from the design of (bio) sensors to bioimaging [22,23]. Considering the unique features of fluorescent CDs and AuNCs, it is of great significance to integrate these two nanoparticles to construct RF probes for accurate quantitative and visual qualitative assays.

So far, to the best of our knowledge, none of the fluorescence nanomaterials have been used as a probe for the detection of DBH. For this reason, we developed a dual-emission fluorescence nanohybrid, as a biosensing platform for highly sensitive detection of the DBH enzyme. DBH enzyme as a trigger agent can induce the aggregation of BSA capped gold nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) due to electrostatic interaction, resulting in FL quenching of AuNC as a signal reporter at 615 nm. However, the FL intensity of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as an internal reference at 440 nm remained constant. Such a simple design not only affords greatly enhanced detection sensitivity compared to single emissive BSA-AuNCs probe by measuring the ratio of fluorescent emission intensities at two wavelengths (F615/F440), but also displays continuous color changes from pink to violet and blue upon the increase in DBH concentration. Moreover, we prepared portable and simple test strips by the embedding of RF probe on top of the nitrocellulose paper to on-site detection of the DBH enzyme. The proposed single probe was also used for the visual monitoring of DBH in human brain cells. Both the mentioned applications may help in clinical analysis and diseases control.

The proposed sensor combines the outstanding advantage of both ratiometric measurements to highly sensitive quantification and the paper-based test strips as a simple and portable set-up for visual and semi-quantification of DBH. Moreover, the detection method applied in the developed sensor is fast which can be used to the on-site application.

More interestingly, aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching of AuNCs triggered by DBH motivated us to develop another biosensing platform for DBH assay by resonance light scattering (RLS). RLS, an elastic scattering process, happens when the wavelength of scattering and related molecular absorption bands are located very close to each other [35]. RLS has many remarkable advantages, such as high sensitivity, no need of trained staff and simple instrumentation (using a common fluorimeter), which make them promising candidates in many biological applications [36,37]. In seek of promising candidates for RLS probe, it has been proved that the RLS spectrum in the visible region of silver, gold and copper nanostructures is significantly distinguished from background scattering of other particles that originate from localized surface plasmon resonance band (LSPR) [35,38]. Generally, nanostructure size growing caused by aggregation states will be accompanied by RLS signal enhancement [36,39]. This fact was the basis of the developed RLS-based sensor for DBH. To date, there is no report on the application of AuNCs probes in RLS for biological macromolecules sensing.

All commercial reagents were of analytical grade and handled according to the material safety data sheets suggested by the suppliers. Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) dehydrate (HAuCl4 3H2O, 99%), bovine serum albumin (BSA),1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and dopamine hydrochloride were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Dopamine β-hydroxylase (DBH) was produced in the laboratory of biotechnology, Bu -Ali Sina University, based on the protocols that mentioned in the supplementary data.

All fluorescence measurements were performed by a Perkin Elmer spectrofluorometer equipped with a Xenon arc lamp. Excitation and emission slits were set at 10 nm. The UV–Vis spectra were recorded using a diode array UV-8453 Agilent spectrophotometer (USA). High resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were performed on a FEI Tecnai G2 20 TWIN electron microscope. The Fourier Transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed by a Perkin-Elmer model Spectrum GX in the range 400–4000 cm−1.
Rest of the materials and instruments in details are given in the supporting information (SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BSA-AuNCs, N-CDs and BSA-AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid probe
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent BSA-AuNCs probes were synthesized according to the method developed by Xie and coworkers [22]. The synthesis of N-CDs was mainly based on a previously reported hydrothermal method with some modifications [40]. In order to get the binary emissive probe, N-CDs with amino groups were covalently bonded to the BSA-AuNCs by a condensation reaction.
Detailed synthetic protocols are presented in the SI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of DBH in buffer and serum solution
ce:section.ce:para: A typical procedure for detection of DBH was as follows: 0.8 μL of the different stock solutions of DBH were pipetted into the 2.0 mL of the RF (N-CDs/AuNCs) probe solutions in a quartz cuvette. The solution was then thoroughly mixed, followed by recording the fluorescence spectra after 2.0 min. The corresponding fluorescence intensity ratio R0/R was monitored to plot the calibration curves and prediction of spiked concentrations. (R0: (F615/F440)0 and R: (F615/F440) were the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of the RF probe in the absence and presence of DBH, respectively.)

The same protocol was performed for the interferences studies. A certain volume of the stock solution of desired species was pipetted into the mixed solution of RF probe and DBH enzyme under the previously mentioned conditions.

The human serum samples were collected from healthy volunteers at Besat hospital (Hamedan, Iran) with informed consent and were pre-treated to remove any protein interference according to our previous procedure as follows [41]. Different concentrations of DBH were firstly spiked into a mixture of 1.0 mL of the un-treated serum samples and 1.0 mL of acetonitrile to protein precipitation. Afterward, this mixture was centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 15 min, and then the supernatant was used for analyzing in accordance with the DBH assay procedure described above.
All the fluorescence measurements were performed at excitation wavelength 360.0 nm, emission range 360–800 nm and band-slits of excitation and emission were both set as 10.0 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of test papers for visual detection of DBH
ce:section.ce:para: This procedure was extremely simple and cheap. Briefly, the nitrocellulose papers were cut into circles (diameter ≈ 0.6 cm) using simple punch tools and placed on a clean slide. Next, the RF probe was loaded on top of the papers and the resulting strips were dried in a vacuum chamber for 2.5 h. After pipetting different concentrations of DBH on paper strips embedded with RF probe, FL imaging was captured in a dark box using a UV lamp, excited at 365 nm.

The overall procedure for RLS detection of DBH in buffer solutions is exactly in accordance with Section 2.5. In this regard, the relative RLS intensity was considered to plot the calibration curve that defined as ΔIRLS = [IRLS-IRLS0], where (IRLS) and (IRLS0) correspond to RLS intensity of assay system and the reagent blank at 440 nm, respectively. RLS intensity was obtained by the synchronous scanning at λex = λem (Δλ = 0 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTT assay and in vitro fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The cell viability experiment was performed using the MTT assay. The PC12 cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM-F12) with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum, 100 U mL−1 penicillin, and 100 mg mL−1 streptomycin. The medium was changed 2–3 days and sub-cultured when the cell population density reached to 70–80% confluence. The cells were cultured onto a 96-well cell culture plates at a density of 15000 cells per well overnight and then exposed to different concentration of BSA-AuNCs (0, 1.050, 2.100, 4.200, 8.400, 16.870, 33.750 mg ml−1) followed by incubation for 24 h at 37 °C. For in vitro assay of DBH, cells were incubated with 1.050 mg mL−1 nanocluster for 90 min. After that, cells were washed three times with PBS and glass slide was taken out and images of cells were collected under microscope.
The detailed procedure for the cell culture and cellular bioimaging of neuroblastic adrenal medulla cells (PC12) is given in the SI section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesize and characterization of dual emission nanohybrid probe
ce:section.ce:para: Fabrication of the nanohybrid probe consists of three steps: synthesis of BSA-AuNCs, synthesis of N-CDs, and hybridization of both BSA-AuNCs with carboxyl groups and N-CDs with amino groups via carbodiimide-activated coupling (Scheme 1 ). As a starting point of our study, BSA protein was selected as a reducing and stabilizing template to synthesize BSA-AuNCs by a simple and eco-friendly method [22,42]. In alkaline media, BSA can reduce Au3+ or Au+ via its tyrosine residues and subsequently the cysteine residues would bind to Au+ through strong affinity Au–S bonds [43]. On the other hand, N-CDs were synthesized by a well-known and simple hydrothermally method to reach CDs with high quantum yield and suitable surface functional groups [40].

The dual emissive nanohybrid probe prepared here and its constituents are characterized first by HRTEM. As presented in Fig. 1 , the obtained BSA-AuNCs and N-CDs are sphere-shaped and monodisperse with an average diameter of about 1.0 nm and 1.6 nm, respectively (based on the statistical analysis of many dots, Fig. S1A and B). A further observation in the HRTEM image of AuNCs (Fig. 1A, inset picture) demonstrates clearly crystalline structure with lattice spacing 0.18 nm consistent well to the Au (111) facet [42]. The HRTEM image of N-CDs disclosed that most of them possess lattice spacing of 0.25 nm (insert in Fig. 1B), an indication of high crystallinity. Moreover, the close association of the AuNCs and N-CDs with different contrasts in bright-field TEM image of nanohybrid can be clearly seen, which confirms the hybridized structure (Fig. 1C).

FT-IR analysis was performed to characterize the functional groups of BSA-AuNCs, N-CDs and AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid. In Fig. 2 A, three characteristic peaks located at 3400, 1660 and 1555 cm−1 are in compliance with the stretching vibrations of N–H/O–H, CO, C–N and N–H bending vibrations indicating the existence of amino and amide groups in N-CDs. The peaks observed at 3300 and 1634 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH and –COOH groups on the AuNCs surface [27,42]. The existence and increase about characteristic vibration peak of –CONH group located at ∼1650 cm−1 imply the formation of amido link. Therefore, these results manifest successful covalent bonding between AuNCs and N-CDs.

XPS has also been used to further provide insight to the surface chemistry of AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid. The full scan spectrum of AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid reveals the main elements of C, O, N and Au from their specific binding energies at 285.2 (C1s), 532.3 (O1s), 399.5 (N1s), 85.2 (Au4f), see Fig. 2B [42]. The Au 4f spectrum was deconvoluted into two distinct peaks at 83.8 and 85.0 eV (Fig. 3 B, inset), which could easily be assigned to the Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 signals, respectively. The high-resolution C1S spectrum (Fig. S2A) disclosed three deconvoluted peaks at 286.5, 288.3 and 290.1 eV which ascribed to C–N, -N-CO (amide) and C–CO groups, respectively [44]. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. S2B) revealed the presence of C–N–C (396.9 eV) and pyrrolic type nitrogen (400.1 eV) [44,45]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. S2C) was deconvoluted into two peaks at 529.8 and 530.6 eV, which corresponded to C= O and C–O–C/C–O–H bonds, respectively [42]. The existence of –N-CO and other characteristic peaks provide obvious evidence for the formation of nanohybrid via a condensation reaction. Moreover, these findings are in excellent coincided with the FT-IR analysis of the nanohybrid.

Later, the optical properties including UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were examined. As shown in Fig. 2C (red line), a broad UV–vis absorption band between 320 and 420 nm and a clear shoulder at 270 nm was observed for AuNCs and there is no LSPR characteristics peak between 500 and 600 nm which matched well with literatures [43]. Aqueous solution of N-CDs exhibits two characteristic absorption peaks at 270 and 350 nm, ascribed to π-π* transitions and n-π* transition of CO or CN bond, respectively [33].

However, for AuNCs/CDs nanohybrid, its UV–vis spectrum showed a peak at 270.0 nm similar to BSA-AuNCs and a slightly higher absorbance at 330–350 nm which is caused by the absorption characteristics of N-CDs.

The fluorescence spectra of the AuNCs, N-CDs and the AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid probe excited at 365 nm were depicted in Fig. 2D. Compared with the red curve (AuNCs) and the blue curve (N-CDs), the pink curve (nanohybrid probe) possessed both emissions of AuNCs and N-CDs with the maximum at 615 nm and 440 nm, respectively. The above findings confirm that the probe was effectively prepared. Fig. 2D inset image from left to right demonstrates strong photoluminescence of AuNCs, N-CDs and AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid solutions under the illumination of UV light (λex: 360 nm).

A label-free and novel ratiometric sensor has been established for analyzing DBH based on the selective aggregation of BSA protein-AuNCs triggered by DBH. All the architecture and sensing principles are depicted schematically in Scheme 1. The most likely hypothesis explaining this phenomenon can be described as follows: DBH is mainly expressed in the tetramer form and each monomer subunit consists of 2 copper atoms designated as the catalytic substrate hydroxylation (CuM) and electron storage/transfer (CuM) domains [46]. It is suggested that CuM is involved in bonding with three histidines and one dioxygen while CuH is coordinated to one methionine and two histidines. On the basis of these facts, there is an active site on CuH for electron transfer and complex formation [1]. Up to now, different research works have been reported with respect to fluorescence quenching of NCs in the presence of Cu2+ ions as a result of their binding affinity to the capping proteins [27,47]. On the other hand, many research works exist with respect to BSA interaction with nitrogen atom rich ligands and its binding ability to Cu2+-complexes [48]. So there are two most likely interactions that can lead to fluorescence quenching of BSA-AuNCs. The first one is the strong coordination affinity between the enzyme active core (CuH) and nitrogen-rich aromatic amino acid residues (Histidine, Tryptophan) present in the BSA through the formation of a metal complex. The second one is the protein-protein crosslinking via electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding between amino acid residues present in DBH and BSA which both are abundant in carboxyl and amino groups [49]. Thus, DBH would act as a bridge to link neighboring BSA capping, which results in the formation of AuNCs aggregation.

To further prove this suggestion, by using the variation in zeta potentials, we obtained negatively charged BSA-AuNCs −39.2 ± 4.39 mV and – 31.5 ± 0.833 mV for nanohybrid (see Fig. 3A) that may be attributed to the carboxyl groups of BSA [50]. Once the DBH enzyme is introduced to BSA-AuNCs at neutral pH, zeta potential value switched to −0.79 ± 0.36 mV. It can be concluded that DBH may disturb electrostatic repulsions between BSA-AuNCs and drives the inter-BSA-AuNCs aggregation result in AuNCs fluorescence quenching while no fluorescence change occurred in the case of single N-CDs. In the following, dynamic light scattering (DLS) was performed. As can be seen in Fig. 3B the individual AuNCs possesses a narrow size distribution around 5.61 ± 2.30 nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.8 (red line), while its average hydrodynamic diameters were changed to 58.78 ± 22.1, 91.28 ± 38.3, 122.42 ± 53.88 in the presence of 0.5, 2.0 and 5.0 μg mL−1, respectively. It was clear that the reason for much bigger DLS size measurements in comparison to TEM might be due to the hydration effect of AuNCs. The results were in well compliance well with TEM pictures which further implies the effective aggregation of AuNCs caused by the DBH enzyme.
Taken all together, it is suggested that the RF probe prepared here could efficiently be applied as a fluorescence probe to detect DBH.

The effects of pH and nanohybrid probe concentration on the system were further examined. To achieve the optimal pH value for the detection approach, first of all, the effect of pH changes on FL intensity of BSA-AuNCs alone was investigated (described in the SI section).

Afterward we monitored the ratiometric response function (R0/R) in aqueous solution in the presence of a 100.0 ng mL−1 DBH enzyme. It can be observed from Fig. 3C, that with pH being increased from 3.0 to 8.5, the relative fluorescence intensity of the RF probe (AuNCs/N-CDs) enhanced first up to maximum platform at pH 6.0 to 7.0, then gradually decreased until it reached a plateau. This can be explained as follows: the zeta potential of AuNCs at pH 6.0 was extremely negative (−25.8 ± 9.60 mV) in Fig. S4B giving the hint that it has good stability in aqueous solution at this pH. However, DBH is a protein with an isoelectric point of pH 6.6 [51] and it has a positive surface charge at pH 6.0. Thus, in virtue of electrostatic interaction, AuNCs and DBH can place in their vicinity and make the aggregations. At pH ˂4.5, DBH and AuNCs are both extremely positive in charge. So, due to the existence of strong repulsion electrostatic between them, aggregation could not happen. At pH˃6.5, DBH and AuNCs both are negatively charged resulting in a decrease in the RF probe response intensity, as well. Thus, at a phosphate buffer of pH 6.0, the next experiments were conducted.

To explore whether the effect of RF probe concentration, BSA-AuNCs, and N-CDs were mixed together according to certain volume ratios of AuNCs/N-CDs (1:1,1:1.5, 1:2,1.5:1, 2:1) and then ratiometric measurements were performed. As can be seen in Fig. 3D, the best proportion of the ratio FL response and color pertained to AuNCs/N-CDs was chosen as 1:1 which demonstrated dual-modality FL signal in 615 and 440 nm and obvious pink color under a UV lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid in detection of DBH enzyme
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, two kinds of sensing strategies for quantitative analysis of DBH based on AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid was carried out. These include fluorescence ratiometric for naked-eye detection and resonance light scattering method.

The capability of the AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid as a sensor for ratiometric fluorescence DBH assays was first evaluated. Under optimized conditions, the FL responses are monitored upon the addition of different amounts of DBH. Fig. 4 A shows that the fluorescence intensity at 615 nm continuously quenched as DBH concentration increased while the intensity at 440 nm remained constant. Fig. 4B demonstrates a good linear relationship between the ratiometric fluorescence response R0/R (R and R0 were the ratios of the fluorescence intensity of ratiometric probe (F615/F440) in the presence and absence of DBH, respectively) and DBH concentrations over the range 0.02–0.16 μg mL−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0. 993. Moreover, the detection limit (3σ/m, σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements and m is the slope of calibration plot) of this ratiometric sensor was 0.004 μg mL−1 which is much lower than that for all of the previously reported methods for DBH detection.

For comparison, the quenching of individual BSA-AuNCs induced by the DBH enzyme was also quantitatively examined. The plot in Fig. S5A demonstrates that the FL intensity of AuNCs gradually lowered with an increase in DBH concentration. The resultant calibration graph shows the obtained linearity in the range 0.02–0.2 μg mL−1 and the limit of detection of 0.012 μg mL−1 (3σ/m). To confirm the specific interaction of DBH with BSA-AuNCs, control experiment was performed through recording fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs at 440 nm upon the addition of various DBH concentrations under the same experimental conditions. (Fig. S6). These results implied that the ratiometric detection of DBH using AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid compared to single AuNCs probe has higher sensitivity which ascribed to its superior fluorescence enhancement.

To show another extraordinary feature of the ratiometric probe against a single FL quenching probe, visually sensing DBH based on a nitrocellulose paper test strip by a simple UV lamp was performed. As shown in Fig.S5C, it is not easy to distinguish color changes by naked-eye at noticeable different DBH levels. Our proposed ratiometric strategy could solve this challenge by introducing internal color and making a variety color changes instead of changes in a single color brightness. As can be seen in Fig. 4C, by gradually increasing the concentration of DBH, paper-based platform would undergo an obvious fluorescence color change from pink to violet and blue which can be readily observed with naked-eyes. This color change is due to the BSA-AuNCs quenching of the RF probe which leads to semi-quantitative detection of DBH.

Our visual paper-embedded with RF probe strips were prepared easily through physical adsorption of this probe into the nitrocellulose spots, defining as the sensing spots, and were excited by a simple UV lamp at 365 nm. The paper strip-based ratiometric sensor proved as a promising tool for on-site, rapid and sensitive assay of DBH by just pipetting a small volume of it on top of the strips. A brief comparison of various other previously reported methods for detecting DBH is summarized in Table 1 . Compared with those, our proposed ratiometric sensor possesses much lower detection limit and many other advantages like simplicity, being user-friendly, cost-effective, portable and on-site detection.

Resonance light scattering measurements were conducted under the optimum conditions pointed out above. As Fig. 5 A shows, AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid possesses a relatively weak RLS while in the presence of DBH enzyme, gradual enhancing the RLS signal occurred. Such an enhanced RLS trend can be explained by RLS theory [54] which gives the hint that RLS intensity corresponds to d6, where d is the radius of the particle. The AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid shows a maximum scattered light intensity in the range 400–450 nm implying to exact compliance with its absorption band. Fig. 5B shows the recorded calibration graph at 440 nm with linearity in two concentration ranges: 0.1–1.2 μg mL−1 with a calibration equation of ΔIRLS = 39.319 C (μg mL−1) + 15.60 (R2 = 0.950), and 1.2–2.5 μg mL−1 with a calibration equation of ΔIRLS = 309.73 C (μg mL−1) – 302.08(R2 = 0.996) where ΔIRLS is the RLS signal variation in the presence of DBH. The calculated limit of detection (3σ/m) was 0.095 μg mL−1.

Besides, in order to make more confident to the exact role of BSA-AuNCs on the quenching mechanism of fluorescence ratiometric nanohybrid, control experiments were performed. As shown in Figs. S7A and B, the individual BSA-AuNCs probe RLS signals were strongly increased with the consequent addition of DBH amounts in the range 0.2–5.0 μg mL−1 while the sole N-CDs probe RLS intensity was essentially unchanged. Based on these findings, it can be understood that BSA-AuNC probe plays as a principle binding role in this assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of ratiometric sensor toward DBH
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity studies of the RF-based sensor toward DBH was performed in the presence of possible interfering agents such as ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), dopamine (DA), glucose, amylase (Amy), HSA, L-Lysine, L-cysteine, Histidine and some metal ions like Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Co2+ and Cu2+. These agents were individually added into the probe solution in the presence of DBH. The concentration of DBH was 100.0 ng mL−1 and the concentration of interfering species was 6000 ng mL−1 except for Cu2+ with a concentration of 300.0 ng mL−1. Results are given in Fig. 5C. It is obvious that the co-existence of these substances has a negligible interfering effect on DBH sensing, and does not interfere with DBH sensing in real matrix samples. The obtained results from control experiments (figures S8A and B) strongly confirm that the presence of potential interferents hardly any affected on DBH detection, verifying high feasibility of this probe for DBH detection in real samples.

We also examined the selectivity of RF probe by applying a series of mentioned substances (5000 ng mL−1) and DBH (100 ng mL−1) on paper test strips, similar to the DBH detection procedure (Fig. 5 D). It should be noted that Cu2+ with the concentration three times higher than DBH (300.0 ng mL−1) could cause a change in the FL intensity ratio signal as it can slightly trigger AuNCs to the formation of aggregates (Fig.S9). Nevertheless, the quenching efficiency was not as much as DBH at similar conditions because interference of Cu2+ (300.0 ng mL−1) in the presence of DBH (100.0 ng mL−1) could easily be suppressed and no clear changes happened. This is presumably due to the steric hindrance of DBH which is caused by its molecular size and subsequently prevents approaching Cu2+ to BSA-AuNCs. Thus our developed RF probe for DBH monitoring manifests good selectivity.

The AuNCs display red strong emission in the visible region, which endows it with a remarkable advantage in biological detection. Before AuNCs are used for intracellular imaging, their cytotoxicity should be first evaluated [55]. To this aim, MTT assay was performed toward PC12 cell lines with varying AuNCs concentrations)0, 1.050, 2.100, 4.200, 8.400, 16.870, 33.750 mg mL−1). As shown in Fig. S10, cell viability has been maintained more than 90% even after incubation with BSA-AuNCs up to 1.050 mg ml−1 for 24 h. This result confirms that BSA-AuNCs has good biocompatibility and can be considered for nanosensing in living cells. We next loaded the fluorescence probe (1.050 mg mL−1) for 1.5 h in to the PC12 cells to track exogenous DBH levels. As can be seen from Fig. 6 B, once a laser excitation at 488 nm, the PC12 cells displayed an intense red fluorescence from intracellular area proving that the ultra-small fluorescence probe has great cell-permeability and entered into cell membrane within 90 min. As demonstrated in bright field images (Fig. 6A), we found that there are no obvious changes in terms of the cells morphology when they are treated with BSA-AuNCs.

Meanwhile, BSA-AuNC (1.050 mg mL−1) as a red fluorescence probe was added in PC12 cells and incubated for another 90 min (Fig. 6B). The fluorescence intensity decreased upon the addition of 5.0 μg mL−1 DBH (Fig. 6C) due to the presence of strong affinity between abundant histidine from BSA and Cu2+ in DBH. Thus, the results demonstrate that the BSA-AuNCs can report changes in DBH levels inside live cells and may be used for semi-quantitative cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the ratiometric-based sensor in a serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: To further test the practicability of our sensor, the ratiometric fluorescence probe was employed to detect the DBH level in a serum sample spiked with three different known concentrations of DBH. Then analysis was performed by the same procedure for DBH detection in buffer solution. The results are summarized in Table 2 . It was observed that there was a good agreement with the spiked and found values, and the obtained recoveries varied from 105% to 110%, implying there are no drastic interferences in such samples. Thus the developed sensor could be successfully applied for the detection of DBH in serum samples. The assay of each sample was repeated independently in three replicates.

In summary, a simple and inexpensive dual-modality nanohybrid probe comprises red-emitting BSA-AuNCs and blue-emitting N-CDs were synthesized for selective detection of the DBH enzyme. Our sensing strategy was based on fluorescence ratiometric method using the as-synthesized nanohybrid probe allowed for providing a novel paper-based visual sensor by the nitrocellulose paper embedded with the AuNCs/N-CDs nanohybrid probe. This platform offered extraordinary performances including simplicity, fast detection, portability, being highly sensitive, and selective and direct visual sensing by naked-eyes under a simple UV lamp just from color changes. The most fascinating application of the generated platform is convenient from point of view of onsite detection without the need of expensive instruments in clinical analysis and diseases control. Moreover, we were able to see that our ratiometric sensor had a decent response to the detection of DBH through aggregation mechanism. Such a unique feature provided the possibility of applying the probe for the detection of DBH by resonance light scattering.

Niloufar Amin: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Abbas Afkhami: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Leila Hosseinzadeh: Formal analysis. Fatemeh Akbarzadeh: Formal analysis. Tayyebeh Madrakian: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Haidar Saify Nabiabad: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.01.046.

Moassess, One-step synthesis and characterization of highly luminescent nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots and their application as highly selective and sensitive nanoprobes for low level detection of uranyl ion in hair and water samples and application to cellular imaging, Sens. Actuator B Chem. 257 (2018) 772-782.
